title: (180) Pain sensitivity questionnaire differentiates individuals with shoulder pain who demonstrate widespread sensitivity with quantitative sensory testing

assertion.value: (180) Pain sensitivity questionnaire differentiates individuals with shoulder pain who demonstrate widespread sensitivity with quantitative sensory testing
assertion.name: articletitle
assertion.label: Article Title

assertion.name: articlelink
assertion.label: CrossRef DOI link to publisher maintained version

items.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Acute postsurgical pain (APSP) may persist over time and become chronic. Research on predictors for APSP and chronic postsurgical pain (CPSP) has produced inconsistent results. This observational study aimed to analyze psychological and psychophysical variables associated with APSP and CPSP after total knee or hip arthroplasty, and to explore the role of sex.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Assessments were conducted before surgery, 48 h, and 3 months postsurgery, including questionnaires (sociodemographic, pain related, and psychological) and quantitative sensory testing (QST). Hierarchical linear regression models analyzed potential predictors of APSP and CPSP, and moderation analyses evaluated the role of sex.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>
                      The study included 63 participants undergoing total knee (34, 54%) or hip (29, 46%) arthroplasty. Thirty-one (49.2%) were female and 32 (50.8%) were male. APSP (48 h) was associated with impaired conditioned pain modulation (CPM) (
                      <jats:italic>β</jats:italic>
                      = 0.301,
                      <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                      = 0.019). CPSP (3 months) was associated with being female (
                      <jats:italic>β</jats:italic>
                      = 0.282,
                      <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                      = 0.029), longer presurgical pain duration (
                      <jats:italic>β</jats:italic>
                      = 0.353,
                      <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                      = 0.006), knee arthroplasty (
                      <jats:italic>β</jats:italic>
                      = −0.312,
                      <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                      = 0.015), higher APSP intensity (
                      <jats:italic>β</jats:italic>
                      = 373,
                      <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                      = 0.004), and impaired CPM (
                      <jats:italic>β</jats:italic>
                      = 0.126,
                      <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                      = 0.004). In multivariate analysis, these clinical variables were significant predictors of CPSP, unlike sex, and CPM (adj.
                      <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                      <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                      = 0.349). Moderation analyses showed that wind-up ratio (WUR) was a significant predictor of APSP in men (WUR × sex:
                      <jats:italic>b</jats:italic>
                      = −1.373,
                      <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                      = 0.046) and CPM was a significant predictor of CPSP in women (CPM × sex:
                      <jats:italic>b</jats:italic>
                      = 1.625,
                      <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                      = 0.016).
                    </jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Specific QST parameters could identify patients at risk for high-intensity APSP and CPSP, with sex as a moderator. This has important clinical implications for patient care, paving the way for developing tailored preventive pain management strategies.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
title: Sex moderates the association between quantitative sensory testing and acute and chronic pain after total knee/hip arthroplasty
